food_subgroup: Crustaceans — cooked crab dish with sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific term for Black Pepper Crab; FOODON_03302757 (crab dish) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked crab as the closest defensible ancestor; no specific black-pepper-crab composite code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226808007 represents crab as a food substance; no specific prepared black pepper crab concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving is approximately one whole mud crab weighing around 500 g (including shell), yielding roughly 200–250 g of edible crab meat and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, cooked with black pepper sauce — composite estimate based on IMR Malaysia MyFCD cooked crab values and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175180, Blue Crab cooked) adjusted for black pepper sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Black Pepper Crab as a composite dish. Values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD cooked crab (mud crab / flower crab) and USDA FDC cooked crab data, with sauce contribution (black pepper, butter, garlic, oyster sauce, soy sauce) estimated proportionally at approximately 30% sauce by weight of edible portion. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are blended estimates. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and oyster sauce content. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — shellfish and seafood dishes; analogous estimate from low-carbohydrate cooked seafood category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Black Pepper Crab. GI is estimated at 15 based on the low-carbohydrate cooked shellfish / seafood category in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (~3.8 g/100 g), yielding a very low glycemic response. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as (15 × 19 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.85, rounded to 2.9 for the full serving; per-serving GL reported here is for a 200 g edible portion: (15 × 7.6) / 100 ≈ 1.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~520 mg sodium per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of Black Pepper Crab due to its high sodium content (~520 mg/100 g), which can contribute substantially to the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or consuming a smaller portion (≤150 g edible meat) is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~3.8 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black Pepper Crab has a very low glycemic index (~15) and minimal carbohydrate content, making it a suitable protein-rich choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the sauce may contain added sugar and cornstarch, so portion control and sauce moderation remain important.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from butter and elevated sodium from sauce ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Black Pepper Crab provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from crab meat but also delivers saturated fat (~1.6 g/100 g) from butter and high sodium from the sauce; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and request reduced-butter preparation to limit saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Crab is a signature seafood dish of Malaysian and Singaporean cuisine, widely regarded as one of the two iconic crab preparations of the region alongside Chilli Crab. The dish features whole mud crabs (Scylla serrata) or flower crabs that are cleaned, cracked, and wok-fried at high heat in a richly aromatic black pepper sauce. The sauce is built from coarsely ground black pepper, butter, garlic, oyster sauce, soy sauce, and dark soy sauce, producing a bold, pungent, and slightly sweet coating that clings to the crab shell and meat. The texture of the crab meat is firm, juicy, and slightly sweet, contrasting with the intensely spiced, glossy sauce. Black Pepper Crab is a staple of tze char (煮炒) restaurants — casual Chinese-style cook-to-order establishments common in Singapore and Malaysia — and is also served at seafood-specialist restaurants. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~14 g/100 g), moderate in fat (~4.8 g/100 g), and very low in carbohydrate (~3.8 g/100 g), giving it a low glycemic index (~15). Crab meat is a rich source of vitamin B12, zinc, selenium, and phosphorus. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to the soy-based sauce components (~520 mg/100 g). The dish is not suitable for individuals with shellfish or crustacean allergies, and contains soy and gluten from the sauce. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low carbohydrate and low GI profile make it a favourable protein source when consumed in moderate portions with sauce restraint.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Black Pepper Crab as a composite prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD cooked crab data and USDA FoodData Central cooked crab values, with sauce components estimated proportionally. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database low-carbohydrate seafood category. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level match was found in any primary approved source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the edible portion including sauce coating, estimated at approximately 70% crab meat and 30% sauce by weight of the edible fraction. Shell weight is excluded from per-100g calculations. 2. Sodium value (~520 mg/100g) is a composite estimate reflecting soy sauce and oyster sauce contribution; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FDC cooked crab and butter at typical usage levels; trace amounts below detection threshold). 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD source for cooked crab. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD source for cooked crab. 6. GI is estimated at 15 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database low-carbohydrate cooked seafood category); no direct GI measurement for Black Pepper Crab was found in approved sources. 7. Halal status is uncertain as the dish may be prepared with non-halal ingredients at some restaurants; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 8. The input record listed price SGD 108.00 for 2 crabs (~500g each) from George Town Tze Char And Craft Beer, Boat Quay, Singapore — preserved as contextual metadata only and not used as a nutritional source.
